facts.text: Mary Seacole was born in Kingston, Jamaica.
Mary Seacole was born sometime in the present century.
Mary Seacole is a Creole.
Mary Seacole has Scotch blood.
Mary Seacole's father was a soldier from an old Scotch family.
Mary Seacole traces her affection for camp-life to her father.
Mary Seacole's mother kept a boarding-house in Kingston.
Mary Seacole's mother was a doctress in high repute with military officers and their wives.
Mary Seacole inherited her mother's tastes for medical knowledge.
Mary Seacole had a yearning for medical knowledge and practice from early youth.
Mary Seacole was taken in by an old lady who raised her among her own grandchildren.
Mary Seacole was spoiled by her kind patroness.
Mary Seacole practiced medical knowledge on her doll as a child.
Mary Seacole practiced medical knowledge on dogs and cats around her.
Mary Seacole practiced medical knowledge on herself.
Mary Seacole was about twelve years old when she more frequently assisted her mother.
Mary Seacole attended invalid officers and their wives from Up-Park Camp and Newcastle military station.
Mary Seacole had a longing to travel that never left her.
Mary Seacole traced routes to England on an old map.
Mary Seacole accompanied relatives to England while she was a very young woman.
Mary Seacole visited London and was struck by street-boys making fun of her and her companion's complexion.
Mary Seacole remained in England for about a year on her first visit.
Mary Seacole returned to Kingston after her first visit to England.
Mary Seacole made a second trip to London, bringing West Indian preserves and pickles for sale.
Mary Seacole remained in London for two years on her second visit.
A fire broke out in the hold of the ship Velusia on the day after Christmas.
Mary Seacole made an arrangement with the ship's cook to lash her to a hen-coop in case of emergency.
Mary Seacole visited New Providence and collected shells and shell-work.
Mary Seacole visited Haiti and Cuba.
Mary Seacole nursed her old patroness in her last illness.
Mary Seacole's patroness died in her arms.
Mary Seacole married Mr. Seacole.
Mary Seacole and Mr. Seacole established a store at Black River.
Mr. Seacole was very delicate in health.
Mary Seacole kept Mr. Seacole alive through kind nursing and attention.
Mary Seacole and Mr. Seacole returned to Kingston when he grew very ill.
Mr. Seacole died within a month of their arrival in Kingston.
Mary Seacole felt her husband's death bitterly.
Mary Seacole lost her mother after losing her husband.
Mary Seacole turned a bold front to fortune after her losses.
Mary Seacole never allowed herself to know repining or depression.
Mary Seacole succeeded in gaining her daily bread and comforts.
Mary Seacole was a hearty, strong woman.
Mary Seacole's fortunes underwent variations, sometimes being rich one day and poor the next.
Mary Seacole did not think too exclusively of money.
Mary Seacole's home was burnt down in the great fire of 1843 that devastated Kingston.
Mary Seacole nearly lost her life in the fire of 1843 because she would not leave her house.
Mary Seacole rebuilt her house by degrees after the fire.
Mary Seacole gained a reputation as a skilful nurse and doctress.
Mary Seacole's house was always full of invalid officers and their wives from Newcastle and Up-Park Camp.
Mary Seacole gleaned instruction from naval or military surgeons who stayed in her house.
Mary Seacole remained an unprotected female out of confidence in her own powers.
Officers of high rank sometimes took up abode in Mary Seacole's house.
W. H. Russell witnessed Mary Seacole's devotion and courage.
Mary Seacole was the first to redeem the name of sutler from suspicion of worthlessness and mercenary baseness.
Mary Seacole nursed the sick and sought out the wounded in the Crimea.
Mary Seacole performed last offices for some illustrious dead.
Mary Seacole's house was always full of invalid officers and their wives from Newcastle or the adjacent Up-Park Camp.
Mary Seacole sometimes had naval or military surgeons under her roof.
Mary Seacole gleaned instruction from surgeons who lodged with her.
Mary Seacole's adventures carried her to the battle-fields of the Crimea.
Mary Seacole remained an unprotected female from confidence in her own powers, not from necessity.
One of the hardest struggles of Mary Seacole's life in Kingston was to resist pressing candidates for the late Mr. Seacole's shoes.
Officers of high rank sometimes took up their abode in Mary Seacole's house.
Mary Seacole was well known to few officers of the 97th before she joined them in front of Sebastopol.
Mary Seacole knew H---- V----, a good-hearted, loveable, noble officer whose death shocked her terribly.
In the year 1850, the cholera swept over the island of Jamaica with terrible force.
Mary Seacole believed a steamer from New Orleans introduced cholera into Jamaica.
Dolly Johnson, a washerwoman, sickened and died of cholera.
While the cholera raged, Mary Seacole had many opportunities of watching its nature.
Mary Seacole received hints about cholera treatment from Dr. B----, who was lodging in her house.
Early in 1850, Mary Seacole's brother left Kingston for the Isthmus of Panama.
Mary Seacole's brother had established a considerable store and hotel on the Isthmus of Panama.
Mary Seacole had difficulty checking her disposition to roam after her brother left for Panama.
Mary Seacole resigned her house into the hands of a cousin to journey to Chagres.
Mary Seacole did not go to her brother empty-handed.
Mary Seacole's house was full for weeks of tailors making rough coats and trousers.
Mary Seacole's kitchen was filled with people manufacturing preserves, guava jelly, and other delicacies.
Mary Seacole invested a considerable sum in preserved meats, vegetables, and eggs.
The Isthmus of Panama stretches between North America and California.
The journey across the Isthmus of Panama was by no means an easy one despite its insignificant distance.
Mary Seacole thought Navy Bay was a more luckless, dreary spot than she had ever seen.
Three sides of Navy Bay were a mere swamp.
The houses at Navy Bay were built upon piles which rotted regularly every three years.
The railway connecting Navy Bay with Panama was then building when Mary Seacole visited.
Fever and ague were having their own way at Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole stayed only one night at Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole's medicine chest was called into requisition at Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole saw men dying from sheer exhaustion beneath leaky tents, damp huts, and broken railway waggons.
The first stage of Mary Seacole's journey was by railway to Gatun, about twelve miles distant.
Every mile of the fatal railway cost the world thousands of lives.
The railway site was marked thickly by graves.
The mortality among railway labourers was so great that survivors struck three times and had to be replaced by fresh workers from America.
Mary Seacole's suite consisted of a black servant named Mac and a little girl.
Mary Seacole had many packages of luggage.
Mary Seacole wore a delicate light blue dress, a white bonnet prettily trimmed, and a chaste shawl when leaving Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole's dress became red from contact with Gatun clay.
Porters robbed Mary Seacole of several articles at Gatun.
Mary Seacole hired a boat for ten pounds to carry her to Cruces.
Mary Seacole's boat was large and flat-bottomed with an awning and a hammock.
The padrone of Mary Seacole's boat was a fine tall negro.
The boat's crew consisted of four men with marked disregard for clothing conventions.
The boat was propelled by paddles, and poles were used when the river was shallow.
The padrone swore Spanish oaths with great vehemence.
The river Chagres rolled with considerable force between low marshy shores and steep, thickly wooded banks.
The river Chagres was liable to sudden and heavy floods.
The padrone pledged to land Mary Seacole at Cruces that night but could not keep his pledge.
The wind rose to a gale while Mary Seacole was on the river Chagres.
Mary Seacole had to pass a night upon the river Chagres.
Mary Seacole ate guavas that grew wild upon the banks of the river.
Mary Seacole fell into a troubled, dreamy sleep on the boat.
Mary Seacole reached Gorgona at daybreak.
Gorgona was a mere temporary town of bamboo and wood houses.
In the rainy season, the chief part of Gorgona's population migrated to Cruces.
Gorgona looked indescribably damp, dirty, and dull.
Gorgona butchers cut flesh into strips about three inches wide and sold meat by the yard.
Mary Seacole bought a strip of meat for a quarter of a real at Gorgona.
Mary Seacole was landed at Cruces tired, wretched, and out of temper.
Mary Seacole's brother met her on the rickety wharf at Cruces with a kind welcome.
Mary Seacole's brother's house was the Independent Hotel, situated at the upper end of the street.
The Independent Hotel was at a capital site that was above water when the rest of the town was under water.
The town of Cruces was under water twice or thrice a year unexpectedly.
Mary Seacole's brother expressed fears he could not provide his sister with the accommodation he wished.
The crowd from Panama had just arrived at Cruces when Mary Seacole arrived.
Many people at Cruces had no better bed than the store floors.
Cruces was as damp, dirty, and confused as Gorgona.
The crowd from the gold-fields of California had just arrived at Cruces on mules.
Mary Seacole has a prejudice against Americans, which she confesses to.
Mary Seacole is proud of her relationship to people of African descent.
Mary Seacole has seen proof of the horrors of slavery with her own eyes and heard it with her own ears.
Mary Seacole is impatient of the airs of superiority which many Americans have assumed over her.
Mary Seacole has met with some delightful exceptions among Americans.
Americans, even from the Northern States, are often uncomfortable in the company of coloured people.
Mary Seacole deemed it a duty as well as a pleasure to study personal appearance.
Mary Seacole's brother Edward owned the Independent Hotel in Cruces.
Mary Seacole traveled to Cruces with luggage and a little servant.
Mary Seacole found Cruces similar to Gorgona in its dampness, dirt, and confusion.
Mary Seacole arrived in Cruces cold, hungry, and unprepared for the conditions.
Mary Seacole's brother Edward expressed fears about providing her with adequate accommodation.
Mary Seacole initially wished to return to her pleasant home in Kingston.
Mary Seacole slept under a table in the Independent Hotel on her first night.
Mary Seacole never travels anywhere without her medicine chest.
Mary Seacole had recently been familiar with cholera symptoms.
Mary Seacole diagnosed the first death in Cruces as cholera.
Mary Seacole treated cholera patients using mustard emetics, warm fomentations, mustard plasters, and calomel.
Mary Seacole successfully saved her first cholera patient in Cruces.
Mary Seacole worked for days and nights with scarcely two successive hours of rest during the cholera outbreak.
Mary Seacole is pleased and gratified when reflecting on times she has benefited fellow-creatures suffering from illness.
Mary Seacole loves to be of service to those who need a woman's help.
Mary Seacole asks no greater privilege than to minister to those in need on distant shores.
Mary Seacole hopes her remaining years will be devoted to similar usefulness.
Mary Seacole is not ashamed to confess her gratification in being of service.
The Cruces people called Mary Seacole 'the yellow woman from Jamaica with the cholera medicine.'
Many of Mary Seacole's patients could not afford to pay for her services and gave only thanks.
Mary Seacole's best practice lay amongst American store and hotel keepers.
Mary Seacole's worst practice lay amongst native boatmen and muleteers.
Native boatmen and muleteers died by scores during the cholera outbreak.
Mary Seacole experienced scenes of horror during the cholera outbreak that haunted her.
A Spanish doctor was sent from Panama but became nervous and frightened.
The Spanish doctor was not familiar with cholera.
The Cruces people preferred trusting Mary Seacole to the Spanish doctor.
Mary Seacole does not think she has ever known despair or despondency.
Mary Seacole intended to establish a hotel in Cruces.
Mary Seacole remained her brother's guest until she could find a convenient hut for her intended hotel.
A Spaniard who was an old and intimate friend of Mary Seacole's brother died suddenly after supper.
The Cruces people initially suspected the Spaniard had been poisoned.
Suspicion initially rested on Mary Seacole's brother for the Spaniard's death.
There was no doctor in Cruces when the cholera outbreak began.
A timid dentist was in Cruces by accident and refused to prescribe for cholera patients.
Mary Seacole was obliged to treat cholera patients in the absence of a doctor.
The cholera spread slowly at first, then rapidly in Cruces.
Fear was a powerful auxiliary to the cholera's spread.
The Cruces natives were constitutionally cowardly and made no resistance to the plague.
The Cruces natives filled the church and had priests bring out figures of saints during the cholera outbreak.
The Cruces natives did not clean their huts or rid the streets of rotting accumulation during the cholera outbreak.
Mary Seacole's medicinal skill and knowledge were put to the test in Cruces.
Mary Seacole was known as 'the yellow woman from Jamaica with the cholera medicine.'
A Spanish doctor sent from Panama became nervous and frightened at the horrors of cholera around him.
Many patients who could afford to pay for Mary Seacole's services paid her handsomely.
The great majority of Mary Seacole's patients had nothing better to give than thanks.
The best part of Mary Seacole's practice lay amongst American store and hotel keepers.
The worst part of Mary Seacole's practice was among native boatmen and muleteers.
Mary Seacole witnessed scenes of horror in Cruces that haunted her.
Mary Seacole saw almost as fearful scenes on the Crimean peninsula among British men.
A mule-owner in Cruces implored Mary Seacole to accompany him to his kraal to treat dying men.
The mule-owner promised Mary Seacole large remuneration if she saved his head muleteer.
The journey to the kraal was difficult due to heavy rain and flooded fields.
The mule-owner's hut had a roof that scarcely sheltered its inmates from rain.
The floor of the hut was damp, rank turf trodden into thick mud.
Mules were stabled in the same hut as the sick people, and were more valued than human servants.
Mary Seacole felt an almost uncontrollable impulse to flee from the plague-spot but overcame it.
Mary Seacole directed the mule-owner to throw open doors and shutters and light fires to ventilate the hut.
Two patients in the kraal were beyond Mary Seacole's skill to save.
The male patients screamed and groaned like children rather than bearing their suffering like men.
Mary Seacole stayed with the patients until midnight, then left for a short time.
The mule-owner returned to ask Mary Seacole to come back because the men were worse.
The mule-owner brought tall, thick boots for Mary Seacole to wade through the flooded fields.
Two patients died during the long night at the kraal.
Mary Seacole held a dying infant, scarce a year old, in her arms.
Mary Seacole thought more of the dying infant than of the men struggling for their lives.
Mary Seacole prayed earnestly to God to spare the infant's life.
The infant died in Mary Seacole's arms towards morning.
Mary Seacole decided to perform a post mortem examination on the dead infant to learn about cholera.
Mary Seacole was not afraid to use the dead infant for examination.
Mary Seacole believed the infant's spirit would not reproach her for the examination.
Mary Seacole bribed a man to help her carry the dead infant to the riverside and perform the examination.
Mary Seacole used a scalpel or substitute to perform the post mortem examination.
The excitement of the moment strung Mary Seacole's mind up to a high pitch of courage and determination.
Daily scenes of death had made Mary Seacole somewhat callous.
The knowledge Mary Seacole obtained from the post mortem examination was very valuable to her practice.
Mary Seacole used mustard plasters, emetics, and calomel as usual resources for treating cholera.
Mary Seacole applied mercury externally where the veins were nearest the surface.
Mary Seacole dreaded using opium because it incapacitates the system from making exertion.
Mary Seacole gave patients water in which cinnamon had been boiled when they felt thirsty.
Mary Seacole treated one stubborn cholera attack with ten grains of sugar of lead mixed in a pint of water.
Mary Seacole rubbed one patient, a girl, with warm oil, camphor, and spirits of wine.
Mary Seacole never neglected to apply mustard poultices to the stomach, spine, and neck.
Mary Seacole kept patients warm about the region of the heart.
Mary Seacole concluded that few constitutions permitted the use of exactly similar remedies.
Mary Seacole learned that treatment which saved one man would likely kill his brother if persisted in.
Cholera generally showed premonitory symptoms such as giddiness, sickness, diarrhoea, sunken eyes, and distressed look.
Sometimes cholera's crisis was so rapid that there was no time to apply remedies.
An American carpenter died of cholera in less than an hour after showing warning signs.
Convicts in Cruces were assigned the task of burying the dead.
Convicts showed utmost repugnance to burying the dead.
Soldiers were employed to ensure convicts discharged their burial duties.
Victims were usually buried immediately after death with imperfect rites of sepulture.
Survivors were seldom afforded time by authorities to pay last offices to the departed.
A New Granada grandee left his wife and family in Cruces and went to Panama for three days.
Cholera broke out in the grandee's house the day after his departure.
A Spanish lady suffered from violent cholera with no one to help her except a child she retained.
A black priest was praying beside the Spanish lady's couch during her cholera attack.
Mary Seacole had difficulty routing the priest and his worshippers to help the suffering woman.
The Spanish lady died of cholera before long.
The Spanish lady's relatives and servants showed passionate anger and despair at her burial like the poor.
The Spanish lady's corpse was dressed in rich white satin and her head was decked with flowers.
The agitation and excitement of the Spanish lady's death scene affected Mary Seacole more than any previous horror.
Mary Seacole developed symptoms of cholera after the Spanish lady's death scene.
Mary Seacole hastened to her brother's house when she felt symptoms of cholera.
Mary Seacole felt an unpleasant chill come over her and went to bed at once.
Mary Seacole contracted cholera, which she describes as attacking 'perhaps its greatest foe in Cruces.'
Mary Seacole's attack of cholera was a very mild one.
By the next day after contracting cholera, all danger had passed for Mary Seacole, though she felt weak and exhausted.
After a few weeks, the first force of cholera was spent in Cruces.
Cholera lingered in Cruces for some months after its initial force was spent.
The Cruces survivors buried their memory of the cholera with indecent haste.
Old houses in Cruces found new masters after the cholera outbreak.
The great Spaniard married another pretty woman by the same black priest who had buried his first wife.
Every now and then someone in Cruces sickened and died of cholera.
Mary Seacole's reputation from treating cholera established her a considerable practice.
Americans in Cruces gladly retained Mary Seacole as their medical attendant.
Mary Seacole determined to keep an hotel in Cruces in addition to her medical practice.
There was a tumble-down hut opposite Mary Seacole's brother's Independent Hotel available to let.
The hut had wattled sides and a rotten thatched roof with two rooms.
The rent for the hut was twenty pounds a month.
Mary Seacole hung the hut's rude walls with calico of gayest colour in stripes with fringes, frills, and bows.
Mary Seacole prepared the hut to accommodate fifty dinner guests.
Mary Seacole determined her hotel would be simply a table d'hote with no lodgers.
Mary Seacole relaxed her no-lodgers rule once in favour of two American women.
Two American women sent Mary Seacole to sleep with a lengthy quarrel of words.
Two American women woke Mary Seacole in the night to witness their quarrel culminate in a fisticuff duel.
Mary Seacole determined to follow her original scheme of keeping a hotel in Cruces.
Mary Seacole rented a tumble-down hut with wattled sides and a rotten thatched roof for twenty pounds a month.
Mary Seacole hung the hotel's walls with colorful striped calico with fringes, frills, and bows.
Mary Seacole prepared her hotel to accommodate fifty dinner guests.
Mary Seacole determined that her hotel should be simply a table d'hote with no lodgers.
Mary Seacole relaxed her no-lodgers rule once for two American women.
The two American women quarreled, fought, and stole removeable items from Mary Seacole's hotel.
Mary Seacole had on her staff her black servant Mac, a little girl, and a native cook.
Mary Seacole adopted her brother's tariff of charges for her hotel.
Mary Seacole charged four shillings for an ordinary dinner.
Four crowds generally passed through Cruces every month.
The distance from Cruces to Panama was twenty miles.
The journey from Cruces to Panama was fatiguing due to lack of roads and rough country.
For more than half the distance from Cruces to Panama, three miles an hour was considered splendid progress.
Mary Seacole made money rapidly at her hotel in Cruces.
Mary Seacole pressed her brother to return to Kingston.
Mary Seacole's brother died and was buried on the Isthmus of Panama.
Mary Seacole found it difficult to manage unprotected as a female with rough male guests.
Mary Seacole hired a black barber named Jose for two pounds to shave her male guests.
Mary Seacole had a little out-house erected for the barber's convenience.
Americans felt great pleasure and comfort in a clean chin.
The barber's huge brush attracted considerable custom to the British Hotel at Cruces.
Male guests at Mary Seacole's hotel often ate with great lack of decency.
Male guests at Mary Seacole's hotel often inserted dirty hairy hands into full dishes.
The floor of Mary Seacole's hotel bore evidence of the American habit of expectoration.
Quarrels often arose during dinner at Mary Seacole's hotel.
Reckless men at Mary Seacole's hotel rarely stimulated their passions with strong drink.
Tea and coffee were the common beverages of Americans at Mary Seacole's hotel.
Englishmen and men of other nations at Mary Seacole's hotel generally demanded wine and spirits.
Yankees had a prodigious capacity for swilling tea and coffee.
Mary Seacole saw one man drink ten cups of coffee.
Mary Seacole's brother advised putting salt in coffee after the sixth cup to stop people drinking more.
Mary Seacole thought it was only the cutest Yankee who could outwit her.
A tall, thin Yankee guest at Mary Seacole's hotel hid eggshells under the table to avoid paying for them.
Mary Seacole instructed her little black maid to count scattered eggshells and chalk the number on the Yankee's coat tail.
Mary Seacole exposed the Yankee's deception when he came to pay his score.
A nasty expression in the Yankee's eyes dissuaded Mary Seacole from playing more practical jokes on Yankees.
Mary Seacole followed her brother's example and forbade all gambling in her hotel.
Mary Seacole received cases for surgical treatment from the faro and monte tables.
Gambling at Cruces was a business by which money was wormed out of gold-seekers and gold-finders.
Dr. Casey owned the most favored gambling table in Cruces.
Dr. Casey was known to be a reckless and unscrupulous villain.
Dr. Casey had been hunted out of San Francisco.
Dr. Casey was violent-tempered and referred disputes to his revolver.
Dr. Casey's gambling table was always crowded.
Dr. Casey was honest in his way and played fairly.
Lola Montes passed through Cruces bound for California.
Lola Montes was a good-looking, bold woman with fine, bad eyes and a determined bearing.
Lola Montes dressed ostentatiously in perfect male attire.
Lola Montes carried a handsome riding-whip which she used to strike an impertinent American across the face.
Mary Seacole was glad when Lola Montes rode off the following morning.
Miss Catherine Hayes passed through Cruces on a successful singing tour.
Miss Catherine Hayes refused to sing at Cruces.
An English bishop from Australia passed through Cruces with a pretty wife.
The majority of crowds passing through Cruces were quarrelsome and held life cheap.
Many natives of Cruces preferred to use the knife, administering sly stabs in the back.
American filibusters bullied the boatmen and muleteers of Cruces.
Englishmen present at Cruces checked the bullying of boatmen and muleteers.
Englishmen at Cruces were never loath to fight anyone who desired it.
Many people died in senseless quarrels at Cruces.
Mary Seacole gained considerable experience in treating knife and gun-shot wounds.
A poor boatman came to Mary Seacole's window with his ears cruelly split by an American.
Mary Seacole stitched up the boatman's ears.
Threats at Cruces included ear-splitting, eye-gouging, cow-hiding, and the application of revolvers.
Yankees had a strong prejudice against negroes at Cruces.
Negroes at Cruces almost invariably filled the municipal offices and took the lead in every way.
Freedom and equality elevated negroes at Cruces to face their old oppressors boldly.
The soldier-police of New Granada were called in when squabbles occurred at Cruces.
The soldiers of New Granada were a dirty, cowardly, indolent set.
The soldiers of New Granada were more prone to use their knives than their legitimate arms.
The soldiers of New Granada bore old rusty muskets and very often marched unshod.
The officers of New Granada were a few shades superior in appearance to the men they commanded.
Justice in New Granada could be obtained only by lavish bribery.
The native population of Cruces, except the negroes, were very practised and skilful in thieving.
The native population of Cruces preferred dishonest night labour at great risk to honest day's work.
Justice in New Granada was always administered strictly to the poor natives.
Only foreigners could evade justice or purchase exemption in New Granada.
Punishment in New Granada was severe, with convicts sent to Carthagena for terrible imprisonment.
Mary Seacole generally avoided claiming the protection of the law whilst on the Isthmus.
Mary Seacole found the law to be an expensive luxury.
Mary Seacole once took a thief caught in the act before the alcalde.
The court-house at Cruces was a low bamboo shed.
The alcalde at Cruces was a negro reclining in a dirty hammock.
Mary Seacole had attended the alcalde while he suffered from an attack of cholera.
The alcalde rose from his hammock and received Mary Seacole in a ceremonious, grand manner.
The alcalde ordered coffee to be brought to Mary Seacole.
The alcalde had a very pretty white wife.
The alcalde offered Mary Seacole a cigarito.
Mary Seacole left a necessary fee in furtherance of justice.
The thief was ordered to pay Mary Seacole the value of the stolen goods instead of being punished as a criminal.
The thief eventually paid Mary Seacole in pearls, combs, and other curiosities after weeks of hesitation and delay.
When an American was arrested by New Granada authorities, justice had a hard struggle for mastery.
An American was brought to court heavily ironed, charged with highway robbery on travellers from Chili.
An angry crowd of brother Americans abused and threatened the authorities when an American was arrested.
The Americans were indignant that a nigger should presume to judge one of their countrymen.
The alcalde threw himself from his hammock and gave determined orders to his soldiers to check the riot.
The alcalde declared that since the Americans came the country had known no peace.
The alcalde expressed his determination to make strangers respect the laws of the Republic.
The Americans seemed too astonished at the audacity of the black man to offer resistance.
The prisoner was allowed ultimately to escape.
Mary Seacole had a narrow escape from the thieves of Cruces.
Mary Seacole had been down to Chagres for some stores.
Mary Seacole returned late in the evening too tired to put away her packages.
Mary Seacole's little maid woke her to listen to a noise in the thatch.
Mary Seacole was accustomed to hear half-starved mules munching her thatch.
Mary Seacole's maid screamed and cried 'Hombro--landro' when a thief was stealing.
A thief stole Mary Seacole's dress containing her purse.
The thief had a villainous-looking knife in his hand.
Mary Seacole screamed loudly hoping to arouse her brother.
The thief seemed inclined to silence Mary Seacole and took a few steps forward.
Mary Seacole took up an old rusty horse-pistol her brother had given her to look determined.
Mary Seacole snatched down a can of ground coffee to prime the pistol.
Mary Seacole screamed as loudly as her strong lungs could produce.
Mary Seacole was accustomed to hearing half-starved mules of Cruces munching her thatch at night.
A thief stole Mary Seacole's dress containing her purse from her store.
Mary Seacole's brother gave her an old rusty horse-pistol so she might look determined.
Mary Seacole used a horse-pistol and ground coffee to frighten away a thief.
Mary Seacole screamed loudly to arouse her brother when threatened by a thief.
Mary Seacole remained at Cruces until the rainy months ended and the river became too shallow for navigation.
Mary Seacole received invitations to farewell parties from store and hotel keepers before leaving Cruces.
A score of zealous Americans dined at Mary Seacole's brother's hotel on the anniversary of American independence.
Americans at the celebration drank champagne liberally at twelve shillings a bottle.
A thin, sallow-looking American gave a toast to Mary Seacole at the celebration.
The American orator expressed regret that Mary Seacole was not a citizen of the United States.
The American orator expressed regret that Providence made Mary Seacole a yellow woman.
The American orator suggested they could bleach Mary Seacole to make her more acceptable in society.
Mary Seacole rejected the idea that her color needed to be changed.
Mary Seacole stated she would have been equally happy and respected if her complexion had been as dark as any Black person's.
Mary Seacole declined the offer of bleaching even if it were practicable.
Mary Seacole criticized American manners in her response to the toast.
Mary Seacole's brother was young and often ill.
Mary Seacole wanted to persuade her brother to leave the Isthmus but he refused.
Mary Seacole bought a miserable little hut in Gorgona for a hundred dollars.
Mary Seacole's hut in Gorgona consisted of one room only.
Mary Seacole obtained the alcalde's permission to use adjacent ground for a moderate consideration.
An American whom Mary Seacole had cured of cholera at Cruces lent her his boat.
Mary Seacole hired natives to cut down and shape posts and bamboo poles for her building.
Mac and Mary Seacole's little maid filled spaces between bamboo poles with split bamboo canes and reeds.
Mary Seacole's new hotel had a dining-room approximately thirty feet in length.
Mary Seacole's dining-room was hung with colored calico and lit with large oil lamps.
Mary Seacole's hotel included a store-room, bar, and small private apartment for ladies.
Mary Seacole paid her laborers four shillings a day.
Mary Seacole's building cost less than her brother paid for three months' rent of his hotel.
Mary Seacole intended to devote her establishment principally to entertaining ladies and caring for the ill.
Mary Seacole's hotel speculation paid well.
Mary Seacole grew weary of her life in Gorgona despite the hotel's success.
Mary Seacole had no female society in Gorgona.
Most females who crossed Mary Seacole's path in Gorgona were unpleasant.
Many females in Gorgona came from the Southern States of America.
Southern American women showed instinctive repugnance toward Mary Seacole because of her appearance suggesting kinship with slaves.
Mary Seacole made money from the wants of American women despite their prejudice.
Mary Seacole forgot everything except duty when sick women came to her for help.
Citizens of the New Granada Republic had a strong prejudice against Americans.
Many fugitive slaves from the Southern States sought refuge in New Granada and other Central American states.
Fugitive slaves in New Granada rose to positions of eminence in the priesthood, army, and municipal offices.
The people of New Granada respected freed slaves more than their native rulers.
New Granada people were prejudiced against Americans because of freed slaves' influence.
New Granada people feared Americans' quarrelsome and bullying habits.
Many people in crowds to California were of the lowest sorts.
New Granada people dreaded American schemes for annexation.
The American Railway Company took possession of Navy Bay and christened it Aspinwall.
Native authorities refused to recognize the American right to name Aspinwall.
Native authorities returned letters directed to Aspinwall marked 'no such place known.'
Legal authorities in New Granada refused to compel defendants described as of Aspinwall to appear in court.
When Americans crossed the Isthmus with slaves, Cruces and Gorgona people whispered offers of freedom to the slaves.
Authorities in Cruces and Gorgona were not inclined to aid in recapture of runaway slaves.
The New Granada constitution provided that a slave touching its soil would have his chains fall from him.
A young American woman of vicious character fell ill at Gorgona and was left behind with a young enslaved girl.
The young American woman treated her enslaved girl most inhumanly and frequently lashed her.
Gorgona residents broke into the house where the enslaved girl was being lashed.
The enslaved girl was found bound hand and foot, naked, and being severely lashed.
The alcalde of Gorgona was a man of color and of very humane disposition.
The American woman claimed the enslaved girl was her property worth so many dollars.
The American woman said the girl was a child at New Orleans and had been properly corrected.
The alcalde told the enslaved girl she was free to leave her mistress when she liked.
The enslaved girl's baby remained in the power of the American woman at New Orleans.
The American woman threatened horrible torture to the child if its mother accepted freedom.
Mary Seacole and others set afloat a subscription for the purchase of the enslaved girl's child.
The American woman was removed into the interior of the country.
Life at Gorgona resembled life at Cruces.
Down with store and hotel keepers came muleteers, mules, porters, hangers-on, idlers, thieves, gamblers, and dancing women.
Monte-tables were fitted up in Gorgona and plied their deadly trade.
Dancers charmed susceptible travelers in the dirty streets of Gorgona.
Dr. Casey was very nearly getting himself into serious trouble from too great readiness to use his revolver.
Desperadoes in Casey's gambling-hut conceived a plan to put out lights and upset his table to steal money.
Dr. Casey fired his revolver into the dark and hit an unoffending boy in the hip.
Gorgona police carried off all parties they could lay hands upon including Dr. Casey to prison.
The wounded boy was brought to Mary Seacole.
The alcalde was known to be no friend to Americans and owed Casey more than one grudge.
Dr. Casey asked Mary Seacole to tell the alcalde the wound was slight.
The ball from Dr. Casey's revolver remained in the poor boy's hip.
Dr. Casey's friends bribed the boy's father to take him from Mary Seacole's care and send him to Navy Bay.
The boy never returned to prosecute Dr. Casey.
The alcalde hated Dr. Casey but could not resist his money and set him free.
Gorgona lay lower than Cruces and its inhabitants more frequently enjoyed the excitement of floods.
After heavy rains, the river would rise so rapidly that in a few hours the chief part of Gorgona would be under water.
During floods, frightened householders kept removing their goods and furniture to higher ground.
During floods, boats were sent to rescue people surrounded by water.
Houses in Gorgona, not made to resist much wind or water, often gave way during floods.
Mary Seacole enjoyed the fellow's fear too much to help him.
Dr. Casey was bribed by friends to send the boy to Navy Bay to a surgeon.
The alcalde hated Dr. Casey but could not resist his money.
Gorgona lay lower than Cruces.
The inhabitants of Gorgona more frequently enjoyed the excitement of floods than those of Cruces.
During floods, thieves were the busiest in Gorgona.
Mary Seacole's hotel was at the upper end of Gorgona, where floods had rarely extended.
Mary Seacole escaped the flood damage.
A Frenchman near Mary Seacole's hotel grew frantic at the loss of his goods in a flood and refused to quit his falling house.
Fire began at a store by the river-side in Gorgona.
The lazy soldier-police, aided by the Americans, succeeded in checking the fire's progress in Gorgona.
A young woman became a mother during the night of the Gorgona fire.
Mary Seacole handed over her hotel to her brother.
Mary Seacole returned to Kingston from Panama.
An American merchant tried hard to persuade Mary Seacole to delay her journey until the English company's steamer called.
Mary Seacole took passage in an American steamer from Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole travelled with Mac and her little maid.
Female passengers on the American steamer questioned Mary Seacole about her destination and conveyance.
One passenger said 'Guess a nigger woman don't go along with us in this saloon.'
Another passenger said 'I never travelled with a nigger yet, and I expect I shan't begin now.'
Children practised cruelties on Mary Seacole's little servant Mary.
One person spat in Mary Seacole's little servant's face.
An old American lady advised Mary Seacole to quit the saloon.
The stewardess was a tall sour-looking woman, flat and thin as a dressed-up broomstick.
The stewardess refused to help Mary Seacole find alternative accommodation.
An Englishman advised Mary Seacole not to leave her seat.
Mary Seacole lost her temper and called Mac to get her things together.
The captain gave Mary Seacole an order upon the agent for the money she had paid.
Mary Seacole was landed again upon the wharf of Navy Bay at twelve o'clock at night.
Two days later, the English steamer 'Eagle' in charge of Captain B---- carried Mary Seacole to Kingston.
Mary Seacole stayed in Jamaica eight months out of the year 1853.
The year 1853 was remembered in Jamaica for its suffering and gloom.
Yellow fever made a determined effort to exterminate the English in Jamaica in 1853.
Some of Mary Seacole's people fell victims to yellow fever in 1853.
Mary Seacole's house was full of sufferers during the yellow fever epidemic.
Officers, their wives and children were borne into Mary Seacole's house from ships in the harbour.
Mary Seacole had become habituated to death in its most harrowing forms.
Mary Seacole found the yellow fever death scenes more difficult to bear than any she had previously borne a part in.
Mary Seacole had to cheer the death-bed of sufferers and soothe the grief of those left behind.
Nature has been favourable to strangers in the West Indies in a few respects.
The Creoles have an affection for English people and anxiety for their welfare.
The Creoles practise the healing art and seek out simple remedies for diseases.
Mary Seacole is familiar with scenes of suffering and death.
Death is always terrible.
Mary Seacole has seen brave men smile at cruel amputations but die trembling like children.
Mary Seacole has seen people whose lives were spent in avoidance of danger die like heroes.
Mary Seacole has witnessed peace and resignation on many death-beds.
Mary Seacole has stood by receiving the last blessings of Christians.
Mary Seacole has closed the eyes of those who had nothing to trust to but the mercy of God.
The Christian's death is glorious.
A good man is always a worker.
Mary Seacole grew to love a young surgeon in a few short weeks.
The young surgeon was a busy, light-hearted, and joyous man.
The young surgeon fell ill and was brought to Mary Seacole's house.
Mary Seacole grew fond of the young surgeon almost as fond as his mother in England.
The young surgeon showed terrible symptoms of the cruel disease.
The young surgeon's thoughts were never for himself but for those he had to leave behind.
Mary Seacole called the young surgeon 'My son--my dear child.'
Mary Seacole wept over the young surgeon.
The young surgeon sent for Captain S----.
The young surgeon dictated his simple will to Captain S----.
The young surgeon's dog was left to one friend.
The young surgeon's ring was left to another friend.
The young surgeon's books were left to a third friend.
The young surgeon asked Mary Seacole to raise him in the bed.
The young surgeon rested his head upon Mary Seacole's breast.
The young surgeon said 'It's only that I miss my mother; but Heaven's will be done.'
The young surgeon spoke of 'Heaven--rest--rest' and 'Home! home!' before dying.
Mary Seacole has a little gold brooch with the young surgeon's hair in it.
The young surgeon's mother sent Mary Seacole a brooch with a letter of gratitude.
Mary Seacole was sent for by the medical authorities to provide nurses for the sick at Up-Park Camp.
Up-Park Camp was about a mile from Kingston.
Mary Seacole left some nurses and her sister at home to go to Up-Park Camp.
About eight months after her return to Jamaica, Mary Seacole prepared to return to the Isthmus of Panama.
Mary Seacole found Navy Bay but little altered upon her return.
Mr. H---- met Mary Seacole on the wharf at Navy Bay.
Three Irishmen had been killed in a row a week before Mary Seacole's arrival at Navy Bay.
The three dead Irishmen were left unburied as nobody's business.
Mary Seacole went to Gorgona and wound up the affairs of the hotel.
Mary Seacole accompanied her brother to the town of Panama.
Mary Seacole and her brother rode alone on mules with three native guides on foot.
The distance from Gorgona to Panama was not much over twenty miles.
Mary Seacole and her brother started at daybreak but did not reach Panama until nightfall.
The track to Panama ran through sand and mud, over hill and plain, through thick forests, deep gulleys, and over rapid streams.
The road to Panama was sometimes made of logs of wood laid transversely with faggots stuffed between.
Mary Seacole and her brother had to work their way through a tangled network of brushwood and over broken rocks.
Panama was an old-fashioned, irregular town with queer stone houses.
Almost all of Panama's stone houses had been turned by traders into stores.
Mary Seacole opened a store in Navy Bay upon her return.
Mary Seacole stayed in Navy Bay for three months or so.
Society in Navy Bay had not improved much in Mary Seacole's absence.
Society in Navy Bay appeared to have grown more lawless.
Endless quarrels often resulting in bloodshed took place between strangers and natives in Navy Bay.
The Spanish planned a general rising against the foreigners in Navy Bay.
An English war-steamer arrived opportunely to prevent the Spanish rising.
The Americans were well armed and ready in Navy Bay.
The native population far outnumbered the Americans in Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole was not sorry when an opportunity offered itself to do something at Escribanos.
Escribanos was a station of the New Granada Gold-mining Company.
Escribanos was about seventy miles from Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole opened a store in Navy Bay (Colon) and stayed there for three months.
Mary Seacole found that society in Navy Bay had grown more lawless during her absence.
Mary Seacole took an opportunity to work at a station of the New Granada Gold-mining Company at Escribanos, about seventy miles from Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole traveled to Escribanos by little vessel because land communication from Navy Bay was impossible due to thick, dense forests.
Mary Seacole spent some months at Escribanos, the only portion of her life devoted to gold-seeking.
Escribanos is in the province of Veraguas, in the State of New Granada.
Carlos Alexander was the alcalde of Escribanos and was a black man.
Carlos Alexander had formerly owned the mine at Escribanos and sold it at a fine price.
Carlos Alexander was fond of talking about his early life in slavery and how he had escaped.
Carlos Alexander possessed no ordinary intellect.
Carlos Alexander had a white wife and a pretty daughter named Juliana.
Mr. Day was the superintendent of the mine at Escribanos.
Mr. Day was a distant connection of Mary Seacole's late husband.
Mr. Day treated Mary Seacole with great kindness.
The major part of the population of Escribanos, including women and children, worked at the mine.
Mary Seacole often watched the workers at the mine and sometimes wandered about by herself.
Mary Seacole once found some heavy yellow material that she thought was gold.
Mary Seacole kept the location of the yellow material secret from the alcalde and everyone else.
Mary Seacole filled some bottles with the precious dust to carry to Navy Bay.
A gold-buyer told Mary Seacole that the material she found was valueless, not gold.
Mary Seacole threw away all the material she had found in annoyance and shame.
Mary Seacole subsequently had reason to believe that some of the material she threw away was genuine gold.
The landing at Escribanos was very difficult and impossible when the surf ran high.
Mary Seacole witnessed a harrowing scene where a boat with three sailors grounded on a rock.
Two of the three sailors drowned and the third died in the boat.
Mary Seacole made arrangements for a longer sojourn at Escribanos and returned to Navy Bay to lay in stores.
Mary Seacole returned to Escribanos in safety and remained there for some months.
Mary Seacole was pleased with the novelty of the life at Escribanos.
Mary Seacole was busy with schemes for prospecting for other mines.
Foreigners were troublesome in Escribanos and quarrels were frequent.
An Irishman was stabbed by an American named Campfield in Escribanos.
Mary Seacole started off to prevent bloodshed when the alcalde instructed his men to shoot the culprit.
Mary Seacole and Juliana prevented the stabber from being made a stern example of.
The stabber was released for a fine of five hundred dollars.
Twelve sailors, deserters from an American ship, plotted to dethrone Alexander and take possession of the mine.
Mr. Day gained information of the deserters' plan.
Mr. Day surrounded the house where the conspirators were and captured them.
The conspirators were sent down to Navy Bay to be handed over to the Government.
Mary Seacole's medical skill did not rust for want of practice at Escribanos.
The place of Escribanos was not healthy and strangers to the climate suffered severely.
A surgeon sent by the West Granada Gold-mining Company was glad to be cured by Mary Seacole's methods.
Mary Seacole was able to nurse Mr. Day through a sharp attack of illness.
The fare at Escribanos consisted mainly of salt meat, rice, and roasted Indian corn.
Mary Seacole found the native fare not tempting and some of their delicacies absolutely disgusting.
Mary Seacole got on better with parrots than with roasted monkeys.
Mary Seacole could agree with the natives' recommendation of parrots as 'buono buono'.
Mary Seacole sometimes conceded praise to a squirrel if well cooked.
Mary Seacole never could stomach the flesh of the iguana despite it being white and tender.
Iguanas are immense green lizards or moderate-sized crocodiles, sometimes three feet in length, weighing generally about seven or eight pounds.
The Indians had a cruel habit of extracting iguana eggs while the animals were alive and roughly sewing up the wound.
Mary Seacole never could muster sufficient courage to witness the extraction of iguana eggs.
The rivers near Escribanos were well stocked with crocodiles.
The sea near Escribanos had its fair share of sharks.
On land near Escribanos you too often met with snakes and other venomous reptiles.
The sting of some reptiles near Escribanos was very dangerous.
One man bitten by a venomous reptile swelled to an enormous size and bled at the roots of his hair.
The remedy of the natives for venomous bites appeared to be copious bleeding.
Mary Seacole made a journey into the interior of the country following the course of the Palmilla river.
Mary Seacole traveled with a gentleman named Little, her maid, and the alcalde's daughter.
The purpose of Mary Seacole's journey was to prospect a mine on the Palmilla river.
Little carefully sounded the channel of the Palmilla river to ascertain its navigability.
Mary Seacole marked what was most curious in the tropical scenery.
Mary Seacole wondered what motive Nature could have had in twisting the roots and branches of trees into strange fantastic contortions.
Mary Seacole watched with unfailing interest the birds and animals disturbed during the journey.
The Indians showed Mary Seacole a milk-tree and she tasted the creamy liquid it exuded.
Mary Seacole was charmed with the flavour of the milk-tree liquid.
Mary Seacole and her party reached the mine and brought back specimens of quartz.
Mary Seacole left Escribanos soon after the journey and stopped but a short time at Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole came direct to England from Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole had claims on a Mining Company which were still unsatisfied.
Mary Seacole had to look after her share in the Palmilla Mine speculation.
Mary Seacole had long been troubled with a secret desire to embark in a novel speculation.
Mary Seacole found something to admire in the people of New Granada, but not much.
Mary Seacole found very much more to condemn most unequivocally in the people of New Granada.
Mary Seacole found that the comparative freedom and religious toleration in New Granada were owing mainly to the negroes.
Mary Seacole found the Spanish Indians treacherous, passionate, and indolent.
Mary Seacole found the Spanish Indians had no higher aim but to enjoy the present after their own torpid, useless fashion.
Mary Seacole found the Spanish Indians very conservative in their habits and principles.
Mary Seacole found the blacks enterprising and inclined to democracy.
Before Mary Seacole left Jamaica for Navy Bay, war had been declared against Russia.
Mary Seacole longed to witness war.
Mary Seacole knew many regiments in Jamaica that had left England for the Crimea.
Mary Seacole's desire to join the regiments in the Crimea became stronger when she heard of the war.
Mary Seacole used to stand for hours in silent thought before an old map of the world.
Mary Seacole traced the route to the Crimea on a map and all difficulties would vanish.
Mary Seacole's friends thought her project to join the Crimea was wild and improbable.
Mary Seacole resigned her hopes for a time and started for Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole turned her wish to join the Crimea over and over in her mind on the way to England from Navy Bay.
Mary Seacole found herself in London in the autumn of 1854, just after the battle of Alma had been fought.
Mary Seacole's old friends were before the walls of Sebastopol when she arrived in London.
How to join her old friends at Sebastopol took up far more of Mary Seacole's thoughts than the gold-mining speculation.
Mary Seacole threw over the gold speculation altogether and devoted all her energies to joining the Crimea.
Mary Seacole had no friends who could help her in the project to join the Crimea.
Mary Seacole's friends would not understand why she desired to go to the Crimea.
Mary Seacole's funds might, carefully husbanded, carry her over three thousand miles and land her at Balaclava.
Mary Seacole arrived in London in the autumn of 1854, just after the battle of Alma had been fought.
Mary Seacole's old friends were battling before the walls of Sebastopol.
Mary Seacole had friends in the 97th and 48th regiments.
Mary Seacole initially considered a gold-mining speculation on the river Palmilla in New Granada.
Mary Seacole abandoned the gold speculation to devote her energies to joining the army in the Crimea.
Mary Seacole had no friends who could help her with her project to go to the Crimea.
Mary Seacole's funds were limited and might barely carry her three thousand miles to Balaclava.
The battles of Balaclava and Inkermann occurred during the winter.
A fearful storm occurred on the 14th of November.
The hospitals at Scutari were unable to shelter or adequately attend to the sick and wounded.
Mary Seacole had seen much of sorrow and death elsewhere.
Mary Seacole had experience binding up the wounds of Americans and Spaniards.
Mary Seacole describes herself as having strong-mindedness or "judicious decisiveness."
Mary Seacole applied to the War Office for the post of hospital nurse.
Mary Seacole had experience with cholera, diarrhoea, and dysentery from her Panama experience.
Mary Seacole obtained testimonials regarding her professional abilities.
A testimonial was provided by A. G. M., Late Medical Officer of the West Granada Gold-mining Company.
Mary Seacole's applications to the War Office failed.
Mary Seacole transferred her attentions to the Quartermaster-General's department.
Mary Seacole then attached herself to the Medical Department.
Miss Nightingale had left England for the Crimea.
Mary Seacole attempted to offer herself as a recruit to Mrs. H----'s nursing group.
Mary Seacole obtained the Secretary-at-War's private address from Cox's, the army agents.
Mary Seacole waited many hours in the Secretary-at-War's hall.
Mrs. H---- sent a message that the full complement of nurses had been secured and her offer could not be entertained.
Mary Seacole had an interview with one of Miss Nightingale's companions.
Mary Seacole applied to the managers of the Crimean Fund for passage to the camp.
Mary Seacole experienced disappointment when her applications were rejected.
Mary Seacole wondered whether American prejudices against colour played a role in her rejection.
Mary Seacole prayed aloud for help while standing in the streets of London.
Mary Seacole describes herself as impulsive and finds it hard to restrain her feelings.
Mary Seacole decided to go to the Crimea at her own cost and responsibility.
Mary Seacole had known people in Jamaica who had been under her care.
Mary Seacole knew doctors who would vouch for her skill.
Mary Seacole knew a general who had helped her before.
Mary Seacole decided to open a hotel for invalids in the Crimea.
Mary Seacole had cards printed announcing the British Hotel.
Mary Seacole took passage in the screw-steamer "Hollander" starting from London on the 25th of January.
Mary Seacole intended to establish a mess table and comfortable quarters for sick and convalescent officers at Balaclava.
Mary Seacole met Mr. Day in England, who was bound to Balaclava on shipping business.
Mary Seacole and Mr. Day agreed to open a store as well as a hotel near the camp.
The firm of Seacole and Day was established.
Camp wits dubbed the firm "Day and Martin."
Mary Seacole invested a great portion of her limited capital in medicines with the aid of a medical friend.
Mary Seacole purchased home comforts with the remainder of her capital.
The supercargo of the "Hollander" was the brother of Mr. S----, whose death in Jamaica had occurred.
The supercargo gave Mary Seacole a hearty welcome and cheering encouragement.
Mary Seacole is a good sailor and did not experience sea-sickness during the voyage.
The weather during the voyage was cold but fine, with a calm sea.
Mary Seacole developed a conviction of the rightness of her step as the ship drew nearer to the Crimea.
Mary Seacole took an almost childish interest in the signals between the "Hollander" and other vessels.
The ship spent a short time at Gibraltar.
Mary Seacole went ashore at Gibraltar and declined offers of companionship.
Mary Seacole selected a young cicerone to show her the fortifications at Gibraltar.
Mary Seacole watched the strange and motley population at Gibraltar's market-place.
Mary Seacole met two officers of the 48th regiment at Gibraltar who had been in her house at Kingston.
The two officers were surprised to meet Mary Seacole at Gibraltar, bound for the Crimea.
Mary Seacole declined offers of companionship and preferred to explore alone.
Mary Seacole selected a young fellow as a guide to show her fortifications.
Mary Seacole met two officers from the 48th regiment in Gibraltar's market-place who had known her in Kingston.
Mary Seacole was bound for the front during the Crimean War.
Mary Seacole was determined to go to the front despite warnings about hardship.
Mary Seacole was willing to sleep on straw and live in a hut made from packing-cases.
Mary Seacole stopped at Malta during her journey.
Mary Seacole met medical officers in Malta who had known her in Kingston.
Dr. F---- gave Mary Seacole a letter of introduction to Miss Nightingale.
Mary Seacole spent six days in Constantinople.
Mary Seacole had a well-filled-out, portly form.
Mary Seacole wore a red or yellow dress, a plain shawl, and a simple straw wide-awake with bright red streamers.
Mary Seacole successfully retrieved Mr. Day's letters from the Post-office in Constantinople.
Mary Seacole chartered a caicque to carry her to Scutari.
Mary Seacole felt emotional upon entering Scutari Hospital and seeing the suffering soldiers.
Mary Seacole found an old acquaintance, Sergeant T----, at Scutari Hospital.
Mary Seacole knew soldiers from Up-Park Camp in Kingston who were at Scutari.
Mary Seacole helped adjust bandages for patients at Scutari Hospital.
Mary Seacole believed she must be useful closer to the front lines than Scutari.
Mary Seacole presented Dr. F----'s letter to Miss Nightingale at Scutari.
Mary Seacole offered to work for the wounded in return for bread and water.
Mary Seacole was bound for the front in a few days when she met Miss Nightingale.
Mary Seacole stayed overnight at Scutari Hospital in the washerwomen's quarters.
Mary Seacole experienced fleas in Scutari and later in the Crimea.
Mary Seacole employed a young Greek Jew named Johnny as a guide in Constantinople.
Mary Seacole called her Greek guide "Jew Johnny" to distinguish him from other Johnnies.
Mary Seacole hired Jew Johnny to accompany her to the Crimea.
Jew Johnny was Mary Seacole's best and most faithful servant in the Crimea.
Mary Seacole secured passage to Balaclava on the transport ship "Albatross."
Mary Seacole had difficulty transporting her things from the "Hollander" to the "Albatross" due to bad weather.
Mary Seacole crossed the Black Sea safely on the "Albatross."
Mary Seacole arrived at Balaclava and saw the harbor filled with shipping.
Mary Seacole experienced terrible anxiety and fear during a voyage across the Black Sea.
Mary Seacole's companions on the voyage were cheerful and pleasant fellows.
Mary Seacole traveled to Balaclava during the Crimean War.
The Prince sank in a fearful November storm four months before Mary Seacole's arrival.
Mary Seacole initially stayed on board the Albatross upon arrival in Balaclava.
Mary Seacole subsequently took quarters on board the Medora, a government-hired ammunition ship.
Mary Seacole sent letters to friends in the camp of the 97th regiment.
Mary Seacole sent a letter to Captain Peel, who was commanding the Royal Naval Brigade.
Mary Seacole had known medical men in other lands and wrote to them.
Mary Seacole had a patron named Sir John Campbell, who was commanding a division.
H. Vicars had visited Mary Seacole's house in Kingston.
Mary Seacole felt confident about the success of her journey before being in Balaclava a week.
The ship Nonpareil was ordered out of Balaclava Harbour before all stores could be unloaded.
Mr. Day was reluctant to meet with Admiral Boxer on Mary Seacole's behalf.
Captain H---- of the Diamond assisted Mary Seacole in meeting Admiral Boxer.
Mary Seacole had known Admiral Boxer's son in the West Indies.
Admiral Boxer initially objected to women coming to Balaclava.
Mary Seacole remained in Balaclava for six weeks.
Mary Seacole spent her days on shore and nights on board ship during her time in Balaclava.
Mary Seacole's stores were stacked on shore and covered with rough tarpaulin.
Mary Seacole received visitors and sold stores from beneath the tarpaulin.
Mary Seacole's chief occupation was helping doctors transfer sick and wounded to hospital transports.
Mary Seacole did not allow business to interfere with helping the sick and wounded.
Mary Seacole approached the sick wharf on her first day in Balaclava.
Mary Seacole assisted a wounded artilleryman without waiting for permission.
Mary Seacole eased the stiff dressings of a wounded soldier.
Mary Seacole gave tea to a wounded artilleryman who had been hit in the forehead.
Mary Seacole wore a yellow dress and blue bonnet with red ribbons on her first day at the wharf.
The surgeons at Balaclava welcomed Mary Seacole's help with the sick and wounded.
Mary Seacole witnessed rough bearded men cry at the suffering they saw.
Mary Seacole observed men showing Christian sympathy and brotherly love to the weak.
